subject: Expose ocata logging
commit_message:
Expose ocata logging

The current implementation doesn't allow to display the log entries
during the CSIT execution. Besides the usage of another service( e.g.
memcache) into a container is not recommended an can be easily manage
by the Orchestration tool. This change improves the method to build
the Docker image for openstack-ocata service.

Change-Id: I873da15ea9cc855acefbf214cb67ee2507d6b2d1
Signed-off-by: Victor Morales <victor.morales@intel.com>
Issue-ID: MULTICLOUD-214

Patch Set 2:

> (1 comment)

memcached is meant to serve the multilcoud plugin service running in the same container, I didn't design it to be a shared caching service for multiple m.c. plugin services, so scaling out is not an issue.
It is trivial and impose unnecessary burden to maintain seperate containers for the 2 tightly coupling services, I am not convinced to do that.

Patch Set 2:

> > (1 comment)
 > 
 > memcached is meant to serve the multilcoud plugin service running
 > in the same container, I didn't design it to be a shared caching
 > service for multiple m.c. plugin services, so scaling out is not an
 > issue.
 > It is trivial and impose unnecessary burden to maintain seperate
 > containers for the 2 tightly coupling services, I am not convinced
 > to do that.

From k8s perspective these two containers can be added into a single pod, which guarantees that will be run in the same worker. In the other hand, this is a best practice[1] and not a hard rule but personally, I don't think that this can be considered as an exception. Regarding maintenance, the official memcached image[2] is maintained  by the community, they apply security patches and critical updates.

[1] https://docs.docker.com/develop/develop-images/dockerfile_best-practices/#each-container-should-have-only-one-concern
[2] https://hub.docker.com/_/memcached/

Patch Set 2: Code-Review-1

> > > (1 comment)
 > >
 > > memcached is meant to serve the multilcoud plugin service running
 > > in the same container, I didn't design it to be a shared caching
 > > service for multiple m.c. plugin services, so scaling out is not
 > an
 > > issue.
 > > It is trivial and impose unnecessary burden to maintain seperate
 > > containers for the 2 tightly coupling services, I am not
 > convinced
 > > to do that.
 > 
 > From k8s perspective these two containers can be added into a
 > single pod, which guarantees that will be run in the same worker.
 > In the other hand, this is a best practice[1] and not a hard rule
 > but personally, I don't think that this can be considered as an
 > exception. Regarding maintenance, the official memcached image[2]
 > is maintained  by the community, they apply security patches and
 > critical updates.
 > 
 > [1] https://docs.docker.com/develop/develop-images/dockerfile_best-practices/#each-container-should-have-only-one-concern
 > [2] https://hub.docker.com/_/memcached/
I would suggest that you make patches to both heat template and oom to add the memcached service before remove the memcached inside this multicloud container. it would be much easier and safer to prepare another ladder before you remove this one.

Another concern is that having dedicate memcached container implies that multiple multicloud plugin service in the same pod would share the same memcached, this is the unexpected sharing using of the cache, which might result in unexpected error since it is not designed to do so.

 > > critical updates.
 > >
 > > [1] https://docs.docker.com/develop/develop-images/dockerfile_best-practices/#each-container-should-have-only-one-concern
 > > [2] https://hub.docker.com/_/memcached/
 > I would suggest that you make patches to both heat template and oom
 > to add the memcached service before remove the memcached inside
 > this multicloud container. it would be much easier and safer to
 > prepare another ladder before you remove this one.
 > 
 > Another concern is that having dedicate memcached container implies
 > that multiple multicloud plugin service in the same pod would share
 > the same memcached, this is the unexpected sharing using of the
 > cache, which might result in unexpected error since it is not
 > designed to do so.

Ok, I understand your concerns as far as I know[1] they can be address with using hostnames in docker-compose file and/or Helm Charts. In the other hand, we're close to the freeze code so makes sense to postpone these improvements to the next release.

[1] https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/topics/cache/
